items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: Why is negative feedback the default mechanism in homeostasis?
items.correct: It opposes deviations from a set point, returning the variable toward its normal value — keeping internal conditions stable.
items.misconception: Negative feedback amplifies changes to make them more noticeable.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback only operates when something goes wrong in the body.
items.why: The word 'negative' confuses students into thinking it amplifies/worsens, but in control theory 'negative' just means 'opposes the change'.

items.q: Approximate energy transfer between consumer trophic levels is:
items.why: Correct answer: 10%. “1%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Eutrophication leads to fish kills mainly because of:
items.why: It's Reduced dissolved oxygen. “Toxic algae” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
